coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots based on endogenous nutrients with visible and NIR fluorescence to penetrate blood-brain barrier

Deng, Tao
Yu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Compared with visible fluorescence carbon dots (CDs), the multi-emission (visible and near infrared) fluorescence CDs based platform (V-N Platform) with blood-brain barrier penetration, real-time brain imaging and drug loading capacities, is of great significance. Herein, a novel type of multi-emission fluorescence CDs (GT) was created to penetrate BBB for brain imaging and local drug delivery, which was synthesized by hydrothermal method, using endogenous l-tryptophan and d-glucose. The capability for in vitro imaging was verified through the experiments on cell imaging. Moreover, the ability of in vivo imaging, especially for brain imaging, was confirmed by experiments in mice and zebrafish. In addition, the in vitro and in vivo experiments preliminarily confirmed its potential to act as a drug delivery platform. This V-N Platform (GT) will be expected to have a significant impact on the researches for CDs and the theranostics of brain diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The basic characterizations. A) TEM image of GT and the inset picture was size distribution histogram based on the image. Scale bar, 20 nm. B) HR-TEM image and lattice fringes of GT. Scale bar, 5 nm. C) Zeta potential of GCDs, TCDs and GT, from the left to right. D) UV spectra of GT, Trp and Glu (The UV absorption spectrum of Glu coincided with the X axis). Embedded graph showed water and GT under 365 nm UV lamp. E) FITR spectrograms, including GT, d-glucose and l-tryptophan. XPS peak diagrams of GT. F) The general layout of XPS, G) C 1s peak diagram, H) N 1s peak diagram, and I) O 1s peak diagram. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photoluminescence properties. A) Visible fluorescence spectrum of GT. Width of λ ex and λ em = 5 nm. B) NIR fluorescence spectrum for GT. Width of λ ex and λ em = 10 nm. C) Fluorescence lifetime of GT and determined by its aqueous solution. D) The pseudocolor images of GT were taken with GFP channel (λ ex = 740–790 nm; λ em = 500–550 nm), cy5.5 channel (λ ex = 630–680 nm; λ em = 690–740 nm), and ICG channel (λ ex = 740–790 nm; λ em = 810–860 nm), using VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager, separately. E) Solid GT performed multi-color imaging: under 365 nm UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm) and cy5.5 channel, from left to right. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Cell imaging. A) HUVEC cells were used. Scale bar, 50 μm. B) HT22 cells were used. Scale bar, 20 μm. C) The scheme of GT co-incubated with cells in cell culture plate. D) and E) Fluorescence quantification of HUVEC and HT22, respectively. Vis and NIR were the fluorescence of GT. Merge1 were the overlap of Vis and NIR and Merge 2 were DAPI, Vis and NIR. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

In vivo imaging and penetrate the BBB. A) In vivo imaging was used to observe the distribution in mice. B) The brain slices of mice were observed by fluorescence confocal microscope. Merge 1 the combination of three channels, and merge 2 the co-localization of 488 nm and 638 nm. Scale bar, 1 mm. C) After microinjection, live zebrafish were observed under fluorescence focusing microscope. D) After injection by caudal vein, in vivo imaging was used to observe GT into the brain of mice. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Drug loading and releasing. A) The process of loading drugs. B) Picture of GT, DOX·HCl, GT-DOX solutions. Under 365 nm UV lamp and taken by iphone. C) Picture of GT, Rh and GT-Rh solutions. Under 365 nm UV lamp and taken by iphone. D) The UV–vis absorbance spectra of GT loaded with DOX·HCl. E) The fluorescence spectra of GT loaded with DOX·HCl. λ ex = 490 nm (width of λ em and λ ex were 5 nm). F) The UV–vis absorbance spectra of GT loaded with Rh. G) The fluorescence spectra of GT loaded with Rh. λ ex = 400 nm (width of λ em and λ ex were 5 nm). H) The release kinetics curves for DOX·HCl from GT-DOX at the pH of 6.4,7.4 and 8.4 in PBS. I) The release kinetics curves for Rh from GT-Rh at the pH of 6.3,7.4 and 8.1 in PBS. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The in vivo drug delivery. A) Fluorescence characterization of GT and DOX·HCl aqueous solutions, under GFP and PE channel. B) Time intervals for taking tissues and organs. C)The ex vivo organs after injection 0–6 h imaged under GFP and PE channel in live imager. (a) and (c) The DOX·HCl group; (b) and (d) The GT-DOX group. D) The ex vivo brain imaged under GFP channel. (a)The DOX·HCl group and (b) The GT-DOX group. E) The ex vivo brain imaged under PE channel. (a)The DOX·HCl group and (b) The GT-DOX group. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Overall schematic diagram for A) chemical synthesis of the multi-emission fluorescence platform and the application in B) bio-imaging, including cell imaging and in vivo imaging, and C) drug loading.
Calculation of fluorescence QY for GT.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots based on endogenous nutrients with visible and NIR fluorescence to penetrate blood-brain barrier

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared with visible fluorescence carbon dots (CDs), the multi-emission (visible and near infrared) fluorescence CDs based platform (V-N Platform) with blood-brain barrier penetration, real-time brain imaging and drug loading capacities, is of great significance. Herein, a novel type of multi-emission fluorescence CDs (GT) was created to penetrate BBB for brain imaging and local drug delivery, which was synthesized by hydrothermal method, using endogenous l-tryptophan and d-glucose. The capability for in vitro imaging was verified through the experiments on cell imaging. Moreover, the ability of in vivo imaging, especially for brain imaging, was confirmed by experiments in mice and zebrafish. In addition, the in vitro and in vivo experiments preliminarily confirmed its potential to act as a drug delivery platform. This V-N Platform (GT) will be expected to have a significant impact on the researches for CDs and the theranostics of brain diseases.

A novel kind of multi-emission fluorescence carbon dots (GT) was created with NIR fluorescence to act as the “V-N Platform”, using endogenous nutrients. With the BBB-penetrating ability and drug loading potential, GT could be expected to with the potential of theranostics for brain diseases.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Various brain diseases, such as Alzheimer's [1] and cerebroma [2], have seriously affected the quality of patients, threatened the life and health of human. While, there is a lack of cost-effective diagnosis and treatment for these diseases, which is limited by the blood-brain barrier (BBB) [3–5]. The BBB mainly comprises of pericytes, astrocytes, basement membrane, neurons, and tight junctions between endothelial cells [6]. Between the junctions and endothelial cells, there is a gap and it is only 4–6 nm [7]. Actually, almost 98% of the small-molecule drugs and 100% of the macromolecular drugs are “rejected” by BBB [8,9]. Thus, the development of diagnostic and (or) therapeutic substances that crossing the BBB is one of the grand challenges.

In recent years, nanoparticles, especially the carbon dots (CDs), have always occupied the attention of researchers because of their excellent optical properties, tiny size (<10 nm), good bio-compatibility and easy surface modification [10–13]. Based on those factors (the nature of BBB and the excellent properties of CDs), researchers have put lots of attention on the research of CDs to cross the BBB. In addition, the biological properties of CDs are extremely dependent on the carbon precursors. For the retention effect, CDs could “inherit” the structure and bio-activities from carbon precursors [14–16]. Thus, it is possible for CDs to overcome the BBB if the appropriate precursors were used. However, it is still difficult to achieve the brain imaging (which is very helpful to comprehend the situation in the brain in real-time), and it is limited by the relative lower epidermal penetration of visible (Vis) fluorescence of CDs [17]. Fortunately, the near infrared (NIR) fluorescence can meet the requirements of deep tissue imaging [18,19]. Therefore, adjusting the fluorescence of CDs to the NIR region for brain imaging is a great challenge, and it is also of great research significance for the diagnosis and treatment of brain diseases.

Glucose, a kind of basic nutrient in the organism, is the main energy source for the mammalian brain. Thus, there is confirmed transporter for glucose into the brain [20,21]. However, try our best to know that there are no researches reported that CDs created by glucose (GCDs) had NIR fluorescence [22–24]. Previously, our team had found the proper doping could bring new physiological functions into CDs [25]. Furthermore, the doping of nitrogen (N) [26–28], phosphorus (P) [29–31] and boron (B) [32,33], etc. have been proved to affect the optical properties of CDs. Especially, N-doped CDs always performed the red-shift of fluorescence emission peak [34,35]. Since N- is an electron-donor group, the incorporation of N atom into the sp2 hybridized inherent in carbon nuclei of CDs would likely form a donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) pattern to expand the conjugated sp2-domain and prompt the red-shift emission of CDs [36]. On the other hand, the participation of N promotes the formation of hydrogen bond, furtherly, expanding the conjugate sp2-domain, which decreases the energy level between highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), then promoting red-shift [36]. Tryptophan is one of the essential amino acids for many biological processes in the brain. Thus, there is special transporter for tryptophan to across BBB [37]. Therefore, the introduction of tryptophan might be helpful to promote CDs to cross BBB and regulate fluorescence for brain imaging.

Consequently, we created a novel type of multi-emission fluorescent CDs (GT) with visible and NIR fluorescence as a V-N Platform, and the design and application were shown in Scheme 1 . The appropriate ratio of d-glucose (Glu) to l-tryptophan (Trp) and reaction temperature were selected to regulate and control the reaction degree. Furtherly, structure of the product was regulated properly, leading the appearance of GT. Then the two applications are as follows: I. Based on the fluorescence properties, GT could be used for multi-color and multi-level imaging. In addition, because of the structure obtained from l-tryptophan and d-glucose by retention effect, GT could penetrate BBB with the ability of brain imaging. II. Due to its surface structure, the potential of drug loading could be predicted. This work is not only with the significance for researches on CDs crossing the BBB, but also contributes to the research on multi-emission fluorescent CDs, especially the relatively rare NIR fluorescent CDs.

L-tryptophan (powder, 99%), Doxorubicin (Doxorubicin hydrochloride, 98%), was obtained from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glucose (d-Glucose Monohydrate, AR), Rhodamine B (powder, AR), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol and sodium hydroxide (flake, 98%) were purchased from Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Rhein (powder, 98%) was from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). 4% Paraformaldehyde Fix Solution were obtained from BBI Life Sciences (Shanghai, China). Certified Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was STR-authenticated by Shanghai XP Biomed Ltd. (Viva Cell, Shanghai, China). Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) was obtained from Sango Biotech (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium Nutriont Mixture F-12 (Ham) (DMEM/F12) and RPMI 1640 were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, USA). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The water used in this work was produced by an ultra-pure-water purification system of Research Water Purification Technology Co., Ltd (Xiamen, China).

Human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) and mouse hippocampal neuronal cell line (HT22) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA). HT22 cells were cultured in DMEM/F12 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and streptomycin/penicillin and incubated at 5% CO2 at 37 °C. HUVEC cells were cultured in RPMI 1640 medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and streptomycin/penicillin and incubated at 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

Healthy female KunMing (KM) mice (6–8 weeks) were purchased from Laboratory Animal Center, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing, China. The nude mice were purchased from Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. All animal experiments were performed according to guidelines evaluated and approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018-0003).

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra was collected on DeNovix DS-11 Spectrophotometer (DeNoVIX, America). The photoluminescence fluorescence (PL) spectra were recorded by a RF-5301 Spectro-fluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was measured by the Nicolet iS5 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Zeta potential was analyzed by Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, U.K.), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The FEI Talos F200X transmission electron microscopy (TEM, USA) was used for morphological images of GT. MTT assay results were analyzed using Varioskan LUX microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to analyze its crystallization by sending sample to the company (Zhong Ke Bai Ce Co., Ltd.) and Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) was used to measure its height. Fluorescence application potential and in vivo imaging of GT was studied using VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea). Cell-imaging results were obtained on confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Leica, Germany) and the software ImageJ was used for the fluorescence quantification. The DAPI stained brain sections were obtained from Wuhan Servicebio Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Wuhan, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GT, GCDs and TCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GT were prepared by hydrothermal method. Briefly, d-glucose and l-tryptophan were weighed in a 5:3 molar ratio (0.4500 g and 0.3060 g), then 5 mL sodium hydroxide solution (0.67 mol/L) was added to preparate GT. The reactions were under 180 °C for 4 h in Muffle Furnace. After cooling down to room temperature, the products were purified by High-speed centrifuge (12000 rpm, 20 min), keeping the dark brown supernatant solution, and they were dialyzed (MWCO 1000 D) for 72 h, followed by lyophilizing to obtain the solid, and were collected for further use.

GCDs and TCDs (Tryptophan Carbon Dots) were also prepared in this paper for the comparison. 0.4500 g Glu and 0.3060 g Trp were weighed and put into Muffle furnace tanks containing 5 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (0.67 mol/mL), respectively. Then they were reacted in a muffle oven at 180 °C. 4 h later, took them out and cooled to room temperature. The products were purified by dialysis bag and freeze-dried into powder for subsequent experiments.

GT (10 mg/mL, 3 mL) was injected into the blank seal engraved with the school emblem of Chongqing Medical University and it would be the printing ink to printed the pattern on white paper. Then, the fluorescence of the printed pattern was observed by UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm) and cy5.5 channel (a NIR channel: λ ex = 630–680 nm; λ em = 690–740 nm).

To calculate the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of GT, the comparative method was used and Rhodamine B (RhB) in ethanol solution, whose QY was reported to be 0.69, was the reference material. UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to detect their UV absorption at 365 nm. After that, their emission fluorescence intensity at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer. The method for calculating fluorescence quantum yield in aqueous solution is the same as above.

HUVEC and HT22 cells were used to assess the in vitro cytotoxicity of GT by MTT assay according to previous reports [38,39]. The cells were seeded into a 96-well plate (5 × 103 per well) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, fresh culture medium (100 μL) containing different concentrations of GT ranging from 0 to 500 μg/mL was added after removing the culture medium and the cells were incubated for another 24 h. Afterward, in the dark, fresh culture medium (100 μL) containing MTT solution (10 μL, 5 mg/mL in PBS) was added and incubated for 4 h. Then, the medium was carefully removed and DMSO (150 μL) was added to lyse the cells. At 37 °C, the 96-well plate was gently shaken for 10 min in the dark. A microplate reader (Thermo) was employed to gauge the absorbance at 490 nm.

The hematotoxicity of GT was evaluated to verify whether it can be injected intravenously. Briefly, fresh whole blood obtained from KunMing (KM) mice was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The plasma and buffy coat layer located in the supernatant were discarded, and the remaining red blood cells (RBCs) were further washed using PBS (pH 7.4) until the supernatant became colorless. 5% RBCs solution were obtained with RBCs suspended in PBS. 1 mL RBCs suspension mixed with 1 mL normal saline (NS) (0% hemolysis) and 1 mL ultrapure water (100% hemolysis) was set as negative control and positive control, respectively. The sample groups consist of 1 mL RBCs suspension, 800 μL NS and 200 μL GT (0.1, 0.5 and 1 mg/mL). All groups were placed at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the absorbance of each group was measured at 545 nm using a microplate reader.

To evaluate the cell internalization and imaging ability of GT, HUVEC and HT22 cells were seeded into a 24-well plate (5 × 104 per well) lined with a cover glass and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, the culture medium was replaced by fresh culture medium (500 μL) containing GT (500 μg/mL) and the cells were incubated for diverse times (1 h, 2 h and 6 h). The blank culture medium (500 μL) was added as control (0 h). Afterward, the cells were washed with PBS three times and fixed with 4% (w/v) formaldehyde for 20 min at room temperature and then washed with PBS twice. The cover glasses were then transferred to the microscope slides and observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) using 488 nm and 638 nm excitation filter to obtain images and quantified by ImageJ.

To assess the biodistribution of GT, six-week-old KM mice (were fasted for 8 h and only water was given, before this experiment) with abdominal depilation were intravenously injected by 150 μL GT at 100 mg/mL. Nude mice used for in vivo brain imaging were injected 150 μL of GT at 100 mg/mL by tail vein. Then different postures (supine and prone, respectively) were used for the fluorescence imaging and pictures were obtained by the VISQUE In-vivo Optical Imager (Vieworks, Korea) under cy5.5 channel at different time intervals.

The zebrafish was experimented to evaluate the ability to cross the BBB of GT. The experiments on zebrafish were performed by Hunter biotech Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). Briefly, translucent Albino zebrafish (4 dpf) with melanin allele mutation were randomly selected and place in 6-well plates containing 20 zebrafish per well. GT at the dosage of 200 ng was administrated by microinjection, and PBS was also administrated as control group. After 6 h at 28 °C, 10 zebrafish in each group were randomly selected and observed under fluorescence microscope and the distribution of GT in the zebrafish was recorded and evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence confocal imaging for brain slices
ce:section.ce:para: KM mice after injected (GT and PBS, respectively) 2 h were sacrificed and the brains were dissected. Then, they were sent to be sliced and stained by Service bio-company (Wuhan, China). The brain sections stained with DAPI were used for analysis under fluorescent confocal.

Water-soluble doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl) and poorly water-solution Rhein (Rh) were used as model drugs. The prepared sample 2 mL (2.5 mg/mL) was mixed with 2 mL DOX·HCl (2.5 mg/mL) and 2.5 mg/mL (2 mL) Rh was added into 2 mL prepared sample (2.5 mg/mL). Stirred at room temperature without light for 24 h, and then dialyzed with dialysis bags against water for 24 h to remove free drugs.

The release of DOX·HCl from GT-DOX was evaluated by a simple dialysis method using PBS buffer with different conditions as the medium. Briefly, 1.0 mL of each formulation containing 417.8 μg/mL DOX·HCl was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 D). The dialytic devices were put in 10 mL PBS with different pH value (6.4, 7.4 and 8.4). At predetermined time points, 1 mL release medium was collected followed by replenishing with an equal volume of fresh medium. Then quantified the released DOX·HCl by the fluorescence intensity. And the release of Rh was the same to that of DOX·HCl. 1.0 mL (850 μg/mL) GT-Rh was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO 500 D) and they were put in 10 mL PBS with different pH values (6.3, 7.4 and 8.1).

The KM mice were intravenously injected with GT (100 mg/mL, 150 μL) and PBS respectively. After 24 h, the mice were sacrificed and the serum was collected to measure the level of alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), creatinine (CREA), urea nitrogen (BUN) and uric acid (UA). The acute toxicity of GT in mice was assessed by measuring the liver and kidney functions.

All the experimental data statistical analysis was performed by Graph Pad Prism 6 and the results are presented as the mean ± S. D. To compared differences between groups, Student's t-test was used. P-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant, conversely, no.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis and characterization of GT
ce:section.ce:para: During the synthesis of GT, the classical hydrothermal synthesis method was used with slight modification in strong alkaline environment (NaOH), which is conductive to the dissolution and dispersion of L-tryptophan (The detail description for optimization and corresponding optical characterizations were showed in the Supplementary Material and Fig. S1, respectively). The obtained CDs solution was dark-brown and the freeze-dried powder was black (Fig. S2). The TEM image (Fig. 1 A) showed GT was zero-dimensional and evenly dispersed. And embedded graph was the particle size distribution and the mean size of the sample, which was about 6.21 nm, fitting with the particle size characteristics of CDs. And the height was about 6.0 nm, measured by AFM (Fig. S3). In order to determine the lattice size, HR-TEM was used (Fig. 1B). Its crystal lattice was clearly and the lattice spacing was about 0.239 nm. And the results from XRD (Fig. S4) showed that there is a peak at 2θ = 8.4, indicating that it has a certain crystallinity. In addition, there is a broad peak at 2θ = 19.84, which confirms that it is with the structure of amorphous carbon. Zeta potential was used to characterized the stability of GCDs, TCDs and GT. And they were about −22.23 mV, −30.83 mV and −29.5 mV, separately (Fig. 1C), illustrating GCDs, TCDs and GT had good stability in aqueous solution. The conjugated structure of GT, GCDs and TCDs was preliminarily determined by Ultraviolet (UV) spectrum. In Fig. 1D, both GT and Trp appeared peaks at around 225 nm and 275 nm. The higher peak at 225 nm was attributed to the π→π* transition in the aromatic sp2 carbons provided by Trp. The peak at about 275 nm was related to the n→π* transition for CO, as in Trp. However, the peak at around 275 nm for GT was wider than that in Trp, and this was because that after the formation of CDs, the bond energy between the ground state and the excited state is disparate, resulting in strong coupling of vibrations, thus a wide spectrum in GT appeared. The absorption moiety at around 225 nm and 275 nm suggested that structure of tryptophan retained in GT. Embedded graph in Fig. 1D was GT under 365 nm UV lamp and showed cyan fluorescence. It could be preliminarily concluded GT was photoluminescent. Fig. S5 showed before being prepared as CDs, there was no UV absorption for Glu and a UV absorption peak at about 260 nm was for GCDs, proving GCDs was synthesized. At about 275 nm, TCDs had an absorption peak and was the same to Trp and GT.

To ascertain the surface structure of GT, FTIR was used. In FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1E), the typical groups in Glu, such as O-H bond (at about 3360 cm−1) and C = O bond (at about 1590 cm −1) were both detected in GT. More importantly, the N-H bond and the C-N bond (at about 1400 cm−1) were found in GT, which proved Trp was successfully doped. In addition, the possible formation for this crystalline CDs was speculated (Fig. S6). Glu and Trp firstly formed polymer-like CDs, and then carbonized to form the amorphous CDs with certain crystallinity [40].

To further determine the composition of elements, the XPS for GT was also analyzed. Fig. 1F showed C, N and O elements were contained in GT, and atom% mole fractions of them were 83.85%, 3.89% and 12.26%, respectively (Fig. S7). And those radically proved the successful doping of N. XPS spectrum also reflected the surface groups for GT. In addition, an unmarked peak at about 500 eV could be Na element in Fig. 1F. GT was synthesized in a strong alkaline environment with NaOH solution as the solvent. After dialysis and purification, it was not acidified when sending samples for XPS detection, leading the existence of sodium carboxylate (-COONa). Therefore, there existed the Na element peak. In Fig. 1G, the XPS spectrum of the C 1s band was deconvoluted into three peaks, at 284.8, 285.09 and 291.6 eV, originating from CO, C-N and –C6H5, respectively. The N 1s band (Fig. 1H) showed two peaks, at 399.58 and 400.12 eV, stemming from pyridinic C-N and pyrrolic N-H groups, which expanded the conjugated sp2-domain and promoted red-shift. The high O 1s peak at 531.44 eV was comparable with carboxyl (–COO–), and peak at 532 eV was -O-H (Fig. 1I). This proved it were N-H bond and O-H bond at 3410 cm−1 in the FITR spectrum for GT. These groups not only proved GT is a kind of N-doped CDs but also predicted the function of it.

Fluorescence is the most prominent and widely used property of CDs (such as bio-imaging). In particular, CDs with NIR fluorescence could be used for in vivo imaging, such as imaging subcutis, muscle and even brain. To determine the fluorescence characteristics, fluorescence spectrophotometer was used. When the excitation wavelength changed from 300 nm to 750 nm, the emission wavelength of GT changed (Fig. 2 A and B), indicating the sample was excitation-dependent, which was the typical characteristic of fluorescence CDs. Surprisingly, two emission peaks appeared in NIR region, when excited by 600 nm (Fig. S1E). In addition, the fluorescence spectrum in Fig. S8 showed when excited by the wavelength from 300 nm to 600 nm, both GCDs (Fig. S8A) and TCDs (Fig. S8B) showed the fluorescence emission peaks did not exceed 540 nm. Therefore, both GCDs and TCDs were only with visible fluorescence. These results showed compared with GCDs and TCDs, GT was an excellent fluorescence CDs with visible and NIR fluorescence, and GT was with more potential in bio-imaging. The fluorescence lifetime of GT was measured using the time-correlated single-photon counting assay and both the visible and NIR were about 20 ns (Fig. 2C), which implied they were from the same fluorescence center. The fluorescence lifetime was enough to weaken or even eliminate the interference of background fluorescence, so as to be conducive to imaging applications.

In addition, the in vivo imager was used to prove the application capability of its fluorescence. The results (Fig. 2D) showed, when the GFP channel (λ ex = 390–490 nm; λ em = 500–550 nm) was used, the purified sample showed a bright fluorescence, implying GT had the potential of imaging in vitro. Moreover, the fluorescence was observed when cy5.5 (λ ex = 630–680 nm; λ em = 690–740 nm) and ICG channels (λ ex = 740–790 nm; λ em = 810–860 nm) were used, instructing that GT had NIR fluorescence, which contributes to the in vivo bio-imaging. Later, the multi-color imaging ability was proved. Fig. S9 showed the photograph of the pattern printed by using GT as the printing ink, under the white light. And in Fig. 2E, the printed pattern showed turquoise fluorescence at 365 nm UV lamp (Vis) and pseudocolor fluorescence in cy5.5 channel (NIR). This meant GT might be of the potential for multi-color coding. Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is one of the most basic and important parameters in the properties of fluorescent materials, representing the ability of substances to convert absorbed light energy into fluorescence. Thus, the QY of GT was detected, and Rhodamine B (RhB) in ethanol was used as the standard reference reagents, respectively. Table 1 showed the QY for GT in ethanol was 0.396, which is higher than that in water (0.177). All those results proved that CDs with visible and NIR fluorescence was successfully fabricated using endogenous nutrients, d-glucose and l-tryptophan. And GT could be expected to be used as a multi-level bio-imaging probe.

In view of the excellent fluorescence property, CDs have always been reported for cell imaging [41–43]. Thus, in vitro imaging ability of GT was also validated. Before the cell imaging experiments, MTT experiments on HUVEC and HT22 were conducted. The results (Fig. S10) showed only the concentration of GT up to 500 μg/mL, the cell viability of HUVEC and HT22 cells were reduced significantly and was about 50%, meaning the cytotoxicity. While, at the concentration from 0 to 300 μg/mL, the cell viability for both HUVEC and HT22 cells was acceptable, not less than 75%. Subsequently, 0.3 mg/mL GT was prepared with the cell culture medium, and HUVEC and HT22 cells was co-cultured with the prepared GT, respectively. The cells incubated at different time intervals were treated, and they were observed and photographed under confocal fluorescence microscope (Fig. 3 C). Fig. 3A showed the imaging of the sample in HUVEC cells, and Fig. 3B was the imaging in HT22 cells. Merge of Vis and NIR (Merge 1) was used to judge whether the observed fluorescence comes from GT or not, so as to eliminate the auto-fluorescence interference of cells. In HUVEC cells and HT22 cells, they showed that when exited by 488 nm (Vis) and 638 nm (NIR), there were bright fluorescence in 1-, 2- and 6-h group, and no fluorescence was observed in 0- hour group, meaning GT was ingested by HUVEC and HT22 cells. The cell internalization could happen in 1 h and last serval hours. Fig. 3D and E were the fluorescence intensity of HUVEC and HT22 cells quantified at different time by ImageJ, which intuitively showed the uptake of GT by HUVEC and HT22 cells increased gradually within 6 h. According to these results, GT could be ingested by somatic and neuron cells. And GT could be used as a fluorescent probe for cell imaging.

KunMing (KM) mice were used to study the in vivo distribution of GT, since KM mice have been widely applied as the in vivo model to investigate the treatment of diverse diseases due to their genetic homology to humans. Before injection, hemolysis was tested. The results showed GT hardly caused any hemolysis (Fig. S11) and the intravenous injection was allowed. Then, the biodistribution on mice was developed and it could be observed in real time, that GT entered into the liver and spleen of mouse after half an hour, and could carried out the hepato-intestinal circulation (Fig. 4 A). It was 2 h after injection that the fluorescence in the body showing the brightest, meaning the GT concentration reached the top at this time. After 8 h, GT was still observed in the body, indicating the slowly metabolize. This phenomenon was beneficial for the fluorescent probe that used as a long-term imaging in vivo.

Besides, the in vivo distribution of GT was also observed by imaging tissues in vitro. Since GT reached the maximum concentration 2 h after injection, other mice were sacrificed after injected GT and PBS for 2 h, respectively. Then, the main organs were dissected for in vitro imaging (using cy5.5 channel) to furtherly confirm the biological distribution in vivo. According to Fig. S12A, it could be known that except liver and spleen, GT also entered the kidneys, it suggested we that GT was expected to be embellished as the kidney-targeted fluorescent probe and drug carrier for the diagnosis and treatment of kidney diseases. In addition, no fluorescence was found in the heart, indicating that GT did not enter the heart and was safe for the heart. More importantly, fluorescence was found in the isolated brain of GT group (Fig. S12B), which meant that GT penetrated the blood-brain barrier (BBB).

To verify the ability of GT to cross the BBB, 2 h after being injected with GT and PBS respectively, the mice were sacrificed, and the brains sliced and stained by DAPI in Service bio-company (Wuhan, China) were observed under confocal fluorescence microscope (Fig. 4B). Merge 1 on PBS group showed the fluorescence of DAPI, while the fluorescence from GT was found in brain slices of GT group, which meant GT had passed through the BBB. Besides, compared with PBS group, the overlap of green and red fluorescence was only found in the Merge 2 of GT group. It determined the fluorescence (except DAPI) we observed in Merge 1 of GT group did be from GT. In order to obtain the sufficient evidence for its ability to cross BBB and image in the brain, the experiments on zebrafish were conducted, with the assistance of Hunter biotech Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). The results from Hunter showed compared with PBS group, bright (Vis and NIR) fluorescence from GT was observed in zebrafish brain in GT group, after 6 h injected (Fig. 4C). GT passed through BBB, and it was mainly concentrated in the hindbrain and midbrain of zebrafish. To further test its ability of brain imaging in mice, nude mice given GT were taken images under the imaging system by prone position. GT began to enter the brain in 1.5 h and a little remained after 5 h (Fig. 4D), which proved GT could cross BBB and performed brain imaging in mice. In addition, it was retained in the brain for about 4 h and it might be beneficial for the diagnosis and treatment of brain diseases.

According to the report [44], the CDs created by using Trp were transported into the brain through tryptophan receptors on the BBB, and it was because of the tryptophan structure on surface. It had also been reported that CDs with BBB-penetrating ability were constructed using d-glucose and l-aspartic acid, and glucose receptors on the BBB contributed [45]. Based on these, we speculated the mechanism for penetrating the BBB might be related to the carrier-mediated transport [46,47]. And we conducted some experiments to preliminarily verify this conjecture. We designed an experiment in which GT competed with glucose (Glu) and tryptophan (Trp) for the transporters in the BBB. The concentrations of glucose and tryptophan were respectively set according to the clinical settings of 10% glucose injection and 1% tryptophan injection. The dosage of GT for each group was 50 μL. As shown in Fig. S13A, from the left to right, there were Trp + GT group, Glu + GT group and individual GT group. And the fluorescence intensity (FL) of the brain in GT group was the strongest compared with the group injected with Glu or Trp after 2 h injection, which indicated that CDs in GT group entered the brain were in the most amount. GT was injected alone, and there was less competitive inhibition between GT with Glu and(or) Trp. Thus, it might imply that GT was transported into the BBB through Glu and Trp carrier proteins. We used ROI to quantify the brain fluorescence in each group. As shown in Fig. S13B, there was a significant difference between GT group and GT + Trp group, but there was no significant difference between GT group and GT + Glu group. Perhaps, this hinted that the transport was more related to Trp receptor.

CDs, being the photoluminescent carrier, allow the monitoring of drug release and contributes to imaging guided drug deliver. Thus, the potential to be constructed as a drug carrier for GT was verified through two model drugs, doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl) and Rhein (Rh), since DOX·HCl is one of the most common antineoplastic drugs and Rh is a multi-pharmacological effects drug with poor water solubility. Fig. 5 A showed the process to load model drugs onto GT. After DOX·HCl and Rh were loaded on GT, respectively, GT-DOX solution turned into brick-red and GT-Rh was sallow, under the white light (Fig. S14). The fluorescence color of GT-DOX group was pink purple, under 365 nm UV lamp, which changed significantly, compared with cyan GT and bright orange DOX·HCl (Fig. 5B). And GT-Rh was light yellow, while Rh was pale brown, under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 5C). Compared with the pure GT, the GT-DOX had UV absorption at ∼470 nm, which is the same to DOX·HCl (Fig. 5D). And in Fig. 5E, the UV absorption peak for pure GT was around 275 nm and peaks about 430 nm was for Rh. However, in GT-Rh there was a peak at ∼280 nm and another was about 430 nm, showing GT-Rh had the UV absorption peak for both GT and Rh. When excited by ∼490 nm wavelength, the fluorescence spectrum of GT showed a single emission peak at about ∼530 nm, while GT-DOX had two emission peaks, which was the same to DOX·HCl (Fig. 5F). Furthermore, when excited by 300 nm, the emission peaks (∼360 nm and ∼440 nm) from GT could be found in GT-DOX (Fig. S15). The peak at ∼530 nm from GT was not detected in GT-DOX, might be related to Ⅰ. After loading, the emission peak at ∼530 nm of GT was disappeared. Ⅱ. The emission peak position of GT was red shifted to the first emission peak position of DOX·HCl, and they overlapped. In addition, the fluorescence emission peak of GT-Rh was blue-shifted, compared with Rh, while it was red-shift when compared with GT (Fig. 5G). These proved both DOX·HCl and Rh were successfully loaded. The FT-IR spectroscopy was also used to characterize the changes of chemical bonds before and after drug loading (Fig. S16A). The results showed that no new bond was formed, suggesting that the drug was loaded physically. In addition, TEM images before and after drug loading showed that the particle size did not change significantly (Fig. S16C). The possible causes for DOX·HCl and Rh, drugs with different water solubility, to be loaded were as follows. I. The zeta-potential measurements showed GT has negative surface potential, while it is known that DOX·HCl is positively charged when it was in acidic and neutral solutions since the pKa of DOX·HCl was about 8.6. Therefore, it can be loaded on the surface of GT via electrostatic interaction and π-π stacking [48,49]. II. The groups in GT, such as amino-group and benzene ring, are hydrophobic, causing the hydrophobic interactions between GT and hydrophobic drugs [50], such as Rh. In this study, expect the hydrophobic interactions, ionic interaction between carboxylic groups or phenol hydroxyl groups in Rh and amine-group in GT [51], enhanced the combination between GT and Rh, leading Rh more difficult to release. And it was consistent with the higher LR value and less release rate of Rh. In addition, GT is the CDs with a large specific surface area. Thus, small molecule drugs might be adsorbed when stirred with GT. According to the research [52], NaCl, SDS and urea were added to break the electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, and hydrogen bonds between GT and drugs, respectively. Fig. S16B showed the addition of NaCl, SDS and urea had effect on the UV–vis absorbance for GT-DOX and GT-Rh solutions, indicating the electrostatic adsorption, hydrophobic interaction and hydrogen bonding contributed to the drug loading.

Then, the drug loading rates (LRs) for DOX·HCl and Rh were calculated by the standard curve of DOX·HCl fluorescence (Fig. S17) and Rh (Fig. S18), respectively. And LRs for them were ∼33.42% and ∼68%, respectively. It is worth noting that the LR value of Rh was markedly higher than that of hydrophilic DOX·HCl. And these results confirmed GT could act as a drug carrier for agents with different water-solubility.

Except the drug loading, the releasing of drug is also crucial for the drug carrier. To determine the release of loaded drugs (DOX·HCl and Rh) from GT at different pH values, the established standard fluorescence curves of free drugs for drug loading were used, and then measured the fluorescence of drugs released from GT at different times. The accumulate release curve of DOX·HCl (Fig. 5H) showed the release of DOX·HCl form GT-DOX was pH dependent, and it was easier to release under acidic conditions. When the pH of the buffer solution was 6.4, the cumulative release could reach 62.5%. As the pH of the buffer solution raised to 7.4 and 8.4, this value gradually decreased to 34.6% and 24.5%, respectively. This phenomenon may be beneficial to the treatment of tumors, since the tumor microenvironment always shows low pH value. As to the release of Rh from GT-Rh, there was no obvious pH response (Fig. 5I). In the buffer solutions with different pH values, the cumulative release was about 41.2%–47.5%. Under acidic conditions, the release of Rh was much less than that of DOX·HCl. In addition, it was considered that GT was not suitable for delivering drugs like Rh, for Rh could also be rapidly released from GT at pH 7.4, which meant that Rh might be transmitted to the whole body with the blood, causing greater toxic and side effects. The drug release properties of GT were related to the loaded drugs, closely, and it's important to choose suitable drugs.

The in vivo experiments were carried to evaluate the delivery of brain drugs. And the results were verified by in vitro imaging on tissues. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX·HCl) was used as the model drug, since it is widely used clinically. In Fig. 6 A(a), the obvious fluorescence of GT could be found under the GFP channel in Live Imager, which is usually used for in vitro tissue imaging; while Fig. 6A(c) showed it was no fluorescence under the PE channel (λ ex = 530–570 nm, λ em = 575–640 nm). In addition, according to the fluorescence property of DOX·HCl, its fluorescence could only be obviously observed in the PE channel (Fig. 6A(b) and (d)). Therefore, GFP and PE channels were used to detect the fluorescence of the in vitro tissues, after DOX·HCl and GT-DOX were respectively injected. According to the brain imaging results, we set four time points: 0-h, 0.5 h, 2 h and 6 h for the experiment (Fig. 6B). Then, the main organs, including brains were took off to perform the ex vivo imaging. In Fig. 6C(a) no fluorescence was found in the DOX·HCl group, when GFP channel was used; while in the GT-DOX group the fluorescence could be observed and it was mainly distributed in liver and brain, after injection 0.5 h and 2 h (Fig. 6C(b)). Furtherly, the PE channel was used to detect the fluorescence of two groups. And the results from Fig. 6C(c) and (d) showed both DOX·HCl and GT-DOX group could observe the fluorescence in the liver, after injection 0.5 h–2 h. Importantly, compared with the DOX·HCl group, the fluorescence also could be found in the brain of GT-DOX group, and this implying that DOX·HCl was delivered into the brain by GT carried. We furtherly used GFP and PE channels to detect the fluorescence of the brain in two groups. As shown in Fig. 6D, when GFP channel used there was no fluorescence in DOX·HCl, but in GT-DOX group there existed the fluorescence. And the same result was obtained when PE channel was used (Fig. 6E). In a word, from these results, we could draw a preliminary conclusion that GT could deliver DOX·HCl to the brain, and GT had the potential to be a drug delivery platform for crossing the BBB.

Finally, the histocompatibility of GT was assessed. After intravenous injection 24 h, the acute toxicity of GT in mice was assessed by measuring the liver and kidney functions. As shown in Fig. S19A, no obvious difference was observed of ALT, AST and ALP between GT and PBS group, implying GT was in vivo safety to liver. In Fig. S19B, CREA, BUN and UA, the indicators for renal function were no significant difference between GT and PBS group. These results demonstrated that GT had good biocompatibility, which is extremely beneficial for its application in vivo.

A novel type of multi-emission fluorescence CDs was created using endogenous nutrients, d-glucose and l-tryptophan. Especially, GT was with NIR fluorescence. GT showed advantage of biological safety and accessibility. What's more, the multi-level imaging ability, especially, penetrating BBB and brain imaging was confirmed by in vitro and in vivo experiments. In addition, the drug delivery potential of GT was confirmed by experiments. Based on these, GT conquered the challenges that CDs are used as multi-level imaging fluorescent probes and medical substances are difficult to BBB, which broken the application barrier of CDs, and provided a strategy to the researches on theranostics for brain diseases.
All the other data supporting the findings of this study are available within the article and its supplementary material files and from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.
